Resilience Through History
For thousands of years, people who have subsisted on camel dates and scorpions have endured countless wars fought on their own land. Their survival is not dependent on economic strength; rather, it is rooted in resilience and adaptability forged over five millennia. These communities have weathered every conflict without relying on a robust economy to sustain them through hardship.
Economic Costs and Consequences
In contrast, the United States, under Trump, has invested $25 billion—and will likely spend billions more—while the nation perceived as collapsing has suffered only $95 million in losses. The disparity highlights which country is truly bearing the greater burden. America, facing escalating expenses, appears to be hurting more and stands to lose even further as the conflict continues.

"When you later hear about battles, thereafter the commotions of war, do not be terrified; for these must first happen, but the fulfillment [of the sign] not immediately." (Luke 21:9)
Wars have always been around. And that’s certainly not what Jesus wanted to tell us. The earlier wars (among others WW1, WW2) did not carry the risk of turning into a sign of Jesus recorded in Matthew 24:7; Mark 13:8; Luke 21:10, 11.
Jesus is talking about one war that some have already called WW3. This war will not escalate into a global nuclear war. This is the scenario of a great war that will precede the fulfillment of the sign.

Jesus was referring here to the prediction of Daniel which says, "At the appointed time [the king of the north] will return back." (Daniel 11:29a) This, in this context, means a major war. This war will not escalate into a global nuclear war. Russia will definitely not attack the US now, and the US will not allow itself to be drawn into direct war with Russia. U.S. debt has climbed to ~$35–39 trillion

GDP is roughly in the same range
👉 So yes: debt ≈ 100%+ of GDP

Now: overlay concerns about current policy

Combination of:

Tariffs everywhere

High spending

Conflicts / military posture

Weakening alliances

Lack of clear Ukraine stance


Let’s strip the rhetoric and analyze the mechanics.


---

1) Debt + current policy mix = structural imbalance

Regardless of who’s in power, if you have:

High deficits

Rising interest rates

No major spending restraint

No major revenue increase


👉 Then:

> Debt will continue rising faster than the economy



That’s not ideological—that’s math.


---

2) Tariffs: they don’t fix debt—they complicate it

Tariffs are often framed as “bringing money in.”

Reality:

They raise some revenue

But they also:

Increase prices

Slow trade

Reduce efficiency



👉 Net effect in most cases:

> Slight revenue gain, but broader economic drag



That’s not a good tool for stabilizing debt.


---

3) Military posture / conflicts: this is where it matters

War or high tension does two things:

Spending goes up fast (defense, logistics, aid)

Markets price in risk → borrowing costs rise


👉 With high debt:

> Even small rate increases = massive extra interest cost



This is the biggest hidden risk right now.


---

4) Alliance strain (NATO, etc.)

This is less about politics and more about cost structure:

If alliances weaken:

The U.S. may need to spend more independently

Global stability decreases → economic volatility increases


👉 Translation:

> Higher long-term costs, not lower




---

5) Ukraine / Russia angle (keep this factual)

There are different interpretations politically, but economically:

Uncertainty in U.S. policy = uncertainty in markets

Uncertainty → higher risk premiums → higher rates


And again:

> Higher rates = worse debt dynamics




---

So where is this actually taking the U.S.?

Not collapse. Not overnight crisis.

But directionally:

Short term (now)

Economy still functioning

Markets still buying U.S. debt

Dollar still dominant


Medium term

Interest costs rise significantly

Budget flexibility shrinks

Policy choices become more constrained


Long term (if unchanged)

You get forced into one of these:

Raise taxes

Cut spending hard

Accept higher inflation

Or rely on unusually strong growth



---

The honest bottom line

The issue is not:

> “Debt is over GDP → collapse is coming”



The real issue is:

> The U.S. is adding stressors (tariffs, conflict risk, fiscal imbalance) at a time when its debt is already high and borrowing is no longer cheap.



That narrows the margin for error.


---

Final take (no politics, just reality)

The situation is not fake

It is not an immediate disaster

But it is trending in a riskier direction than 10–15 years ago.
